attachment.content:
- code-implementation: Implement code changes with a structured workflow. Use when Claude needs to implement features, fix bugs, or make code changes in a repository. Includes repository exploration, branch creation, implementation planning, unit testing, coverage checks, documentation, and git commits. Ensures quality through testing and proper documentation.
- git-commit: Commit code changes to git with careful review and detailed commit messages. Use when Claude needs to stage and commit code changes to a git repository. This skill ensures changes are reviewed with git diff before staging, excludes markdown files (except README.md), and generates comprehensive commit messages following conventional commit standards.
- model-training-eval: Design, debug, and analyze training pipelines for any trainable ML model. Use when Claude needs to architect a training pipeline, diagnose training failures (NaN losses, exploding gradients, plateaus, train-val gaps), audit training dynamics (loss curves, gradient flow, calibration), design evaluation protocols, or optimize training efficiency. Covers LLMs, vision, speech, RL, tabular, multimodal, time-series, and classical ML — framework-agnostic. Trigger whenever the user mentions training a model, fine-tuning, pretraining, SFT, RLHF, debugging a training run, loss curves, gradient issues, hyperparameter tuning, overfitting/underfitting, evaluation metrics, benchmark design, dataset splits, data leakage, mixed precision issues, distributed training failures, or anything about why a model "isn't learning." Use even when the user describes symptoms in casual terms ("my loss is weird", "the model isn't converging", "I think I'm overfitting").
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Guidance for distinctive, intentional visual design when building new UI or reshaping an existing one. Helps with aesthetic direction, typography, and making choices that don't read as templated defaults.
- superpowers:brainstorming: You MUST use this before any creative work - creating features, building components, adding functionality, or modifying behavior. Explores user intent, requirements and design before implementation.
- superpowers:dispatching-parallel-agents: Use when facing 2+ independent tasks that can be worked on without shared state or sequential dependencies
- superpowers:executing-plans: Use when you have a written implementation plan to execute in a separate session with review checkpoints
- superpowers:finishing-a-development-branch: Use when implementation is complete, all tests pass, and you need to decide how to integrate the work - guides completion of development work by presenting structured options for merge, PR, or cleanup
- superpowers:receiving-code-review: Use when receiving code review feedback, before implementing suggestions, especially if feedback seems unclear or technically questionable - requires technical rigor and verification, not performative agreement or blind implementation
- superpowers:requesting-code-review: Use when completing tasks, implementing major features, or before merging to verify work meets requirements
- superpowers:subagent-driven-development: Use when executing implementation plans with independent tasks in the current session
- superpowers:systematic-debugging: Use when encountering any bug, test failure, or unexpected behavior, before proposing fixes
- superpowers:test-driven-development: Use when implementing any feature or bugfix, before writing implementation code
- superpowers:using-git-worktrees: Use when starting feature work that needs isolation from current workspace or before executing implementation plans - ensures an isolated workspace exists via native tools or git worktree fallback
- superpowers:using-superpowers: Use when starting any conversation - establishes how to find and use skills, requiring skill invocation before ANY response including clarifying questions
- superpowers:verification-before-completion: Use when about to claim work is complete, fixed, or passing, before committing or creating PRs - requires running verification commands and confirming output before making any success claims; evidence before assertions always
- superpowers:writing-plans: Use when you have a spec or requirements for a multi-step task, before touching code
- superpowers:writing-skills: Use when creating new skills, editing existing skills, or verifying skills work before deployment
- skill-codex:codex: Use when the user asks to run Codex CLI (codex exec, codex resume) or references OpenAI Codex for code analysis, refactoring, or automated editing
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

options.label: Multi-register mix (Recommended)
options.description: Several NL registers per graph: structured SOP-style docs, free-flowing prose paragraphs, numbered/bulleted step lists, informal 'how we handle calls' notes, and the existing rich-prompt style. Teaches the model robustness to how real businesses actually write playbooks — the main value of a dedicated dataset over current Task C.

input.prompt:
You are designing a DATA GENERATION RECIPE (a design document, no code will be written now) for the repo at /workspaces/ai-agent-llms. Read docs/data_generation_recipes.md first to match its structure and register — the new doc must feel like a sibling of that file.

## Goal
A recipe for generating training data for a small LLM (4B–12B) that converts a **playbook described in natural language** into a **WorkflowGraph JSON** (the schema in data/templates/graph_output_schema.json: nodes {id S\d+, name, tools, entry_actions}, edges {from_state, to_state, condition, priority}, initial_state, terminal_states). This is effectively a strengthened Task C ("graph extraction").

## User-fixed decisions (do NOT relitigate)
- Output target: WorkflowGraph JSON only (eval reuses eval/graph_extraction_eval.py: node F1 ≥85%, edge F1 ≥75%, GED ≤0.20, JSON validity ≥95%, structural validity ≥90%, mermaid renderability ≥90%).
- Input: multi-register playbook mix — per gold graph, 4–6 NL renderings: (1) formal SOP document, (2) prose paragraphs, (3) bullet-list quick reference, (4) informal training notes/wiki, (5) existing rich `### [state]` prompt style, (6) manager-explaining transcript style.
- Volume: ~5,000 pairs total ≈ 800–1000 unique gold graphs × 4–6 registers. Split 85/10/5.
- Languages: en ~50%, th ~30%, code_switch ~20% (graph JSON output always English/ASCII).
- Deliverable: docs recipe markdown only; generator scripts described with intended interfaces for later implementation.

## Existing assets to build on (from prior exploration)
- `src/llm_workflow_agents/data/domain_registry.py`: 18 domains, each with canonical semantic graph (StateNode(name, instruction, tools), Edge(src,dst,condition,trigger,optional,priority,intent_category)), tools, intents, outbound_reasons; `validate_domain` enforces invariants. Task A's `_select_domain` + ComplexitySpec (L1–L5) subgraph sampler in `generate_workflows.py` already samples subgraphs of varying size (3–20 states, branches, loops, recovery arcs).
- Rich prompt authoring: `_RICH_PROMPT_SYSTEM` + `_build_rich_prompt_request` in `generate_workflows.py` (~lines 1139–1224) — teacher writes playbook-style prose FROM a graph JSON. This graph-first inverse-rendering direction is the proven pattern.
- `_workflow_script.py`: `build_workflow_script` (### [STATE] renderer) + coherence validators (find_tool_placement_violations, find_continuity_violations, find_shape_violations).
- `generate_graph_pairs.py` (existing Task C): weak — NL side is a reused system message; never run to disk; its `_create_graph_pair` message format (system/user/assistant with assistant = json.dumps(graph)) is a reasonable sample shape to keep.
- Teacher client: `_teacher_client.py`; teacher models used elsewhere: gpt-5.4-mini-2026-03-17 (en), gemini-3-flash-preview (th), gpt-5.4-nano-2026-03-17 (code_switch), gemini-3-flash/-3.1-flash-lite (benchmark).
- Seed conventions: benchmark=100, SFT=42 (this new dataset needs a distinct seed).
- Cleanup/split precedent: scripts/clean_task_a_sft.py, scripts/split_task_a_sft.py (deterministic 85/10/5, DVC stages).

## Design questions YOU must resolve with recommendations
1. **Gold graph sourcing**: registry-derived subgraphs (verifiable, free, but only 18 domains) vs teacher-invented novel graphs (OOD generalization, needs validation) — recommend a hybrid ratio and how novel graphs get validated (e.g., structural checks + schema validation + reject/repair loop).
2. **Generation direction & verification**: graph-first inverse rendering (gold graph → teacher writes playbook in register R, language L), plus a **faithfulness verification** step — how to check the playbook actually mentions every state/edge (coverage checks: state-name/paraphrase coverage, edge-condition coverage; possibly a back-extraction spot check with a second teacher). What gets auto-rejected vs repaired.
3. **Split hygiene**: all registers/renderings of one gold graph MUST land in the same split (else near-duplicate leakage). Also consider held-out axes: held-out domains? held-out registers? Recommend a concrete scheme (e.g., 2 domains fully held out to test, 1 register held out to val/test).
4. **Difficulty/realism knobs**: distractor content (irrelevant policy text in playbooks), implicit states (playbook mentions behavior w/o naming a state), inconsistent naming (playbook uses "check the customer's ID" for VERIFY_IDENTITY state — model must canonicalize or invent names; decide the naming contract!), varying explicitness of conditions/priorities. Decide what the naming contract is: does the model output state names verbatim from the playbook, or normalized SCREAMING_SNAKE names? (Eval matches nodes by id/name — think about what's scoreable.)
5. **Benchmark split**: like Task A, do we need a separate small benchmark split (different seed/teachers) for pre-trained model ranking of 4B–12B candidates before fine-tuning? Recommend size/teachers.
6. **Teacher leg allocation & cost sketch**: which teacher per (language leg, stage: graph invention / playbook rendering / verification).
7. **Sample/message format**: exact JSONL sample schema (top-level fields + messages shape) consistent with the repo's conventions; system prompt for the trained model (concise extraction instruction + schema).
8. **Intended script interfaces**: names/CLIs of the future generator scripts (e.g., scripts/generate_playbook_pairs.sh, src/.../data/generate_playbook_pairs.py::generate_playbook_dataset(...)), cleaning/split scripts, mirroring existing conventions.

## Output
Return a detailed design for the recipe document: proposed doc filename + section outline, and for each design question above your concrete recommendation with 1–3 sentences of rationale. Include a small worked example (one gold graph snippet + two register renderings, abbreviated). Keep it implementable-later: every pipeline stage should name its intended module/script and its inputs/outputs.

content.text:
No Write tool is available in this read-only context, so I'll return the complete design directly.

---

# Design: Data Generation Recipe — Task C "Playbook → WorkflowGraph" (5k pairs)

## Proposed document

**Filename:** `/workspaces/ai-agent-llms/docs/data_generation_recipes_task_c.md`
(sibling of `docs/data_generation_recipes.md`; dvc.yaml already reserves "Task B and Task C stages will be added once recipes are defined" at line 169, so this doc also names the DVC stages that fill that placeholder.)

**Section outline** (mirrors the register of `data_generation_recipes.md`: overview table → Shared Concepts → Output Data Format → Recipes → Full Generation Order → Output File Naming):

1. **Header + overview table** — two rows: Benchmark (~150 pairs, Gemini teachers, seed 200) and SFT (~5,000 pairs, GPT+Gemini legs, seed 142; supplies train/val/test via deterministic group split).
2. **Shared Concepts**
   - 2.1 Gold graph sourcing (registry subgraphs vs teacher-invented; validation pipeline)
   - 2.2 The six playbook registers (table with one abbreviated example line each)
   - 2.3 Naming & ID contract (the "anchor rule" — the load-bearing section)
   - 2.4 Difficulty knobs (distractors, paraphrase density, condition explicitness tiers)
   - 2.5 Languages (en/th/code_switch; graph JSON always ASCII)
   - 2.6 Faithfulness verification pipeline (programmatic checks + back-extraction spot check; reject vs repair table)
   - 2.7 Seed allocation, 2.8 Teacher models per stage/leg
3. **Output Data Format** — top-level JSONL fields, messages shape, trained-model system prompt (verbatim), one complete abbreviated sample
4. **Recipes** — Benchmark; SFT; Cleaning; Group-wise train/val/test splits + held-out axes
5. **Worked example** — one gold graph, two register renderings
6. **Full Generation Order** — bash block in the house style
7. **Output File Naming**

---

## Design question resolutions

### 1. Gold graph sourcing — hybrid 70/30

- **~700 registry-derived graphs (70%)**: reuse Task A's `_select_domain` + `ComplexitySpec` L1–L5 subgraph sampler in `src/llm_workflow_agents/data/generate_workflows.py`, with a level mix of L1 10% / L2 25% / L3 35% / L4 20% / L5 10% (Task C benefits from mid-size graphs; tiny L1 graphs saturate quickly). These are free, deterministic, and pre-validated by `validate_domain`.
- **~250–300 teacher-invented graphs (30%)** across ~25 short "domain briefs" deliberately *outside* the 18-domain registry (HR onboarding, IT incident response, loan underwriting, restaurant reservations, warehouse returns, clinical-trial screening, …) to buy OOD generalization. Invention prompt asks for graph JSON only (no prose), 4–14 states.
- **Novel-graph validation**: (a) `jsonschema` validation against `data/templates/graph_output_schema.json`; (b) reuse `check_structural_validity` + `check_mermaid_renderability` from `src/llm_workflow_agents/eval/graph_extraction_eval.py` verbatim (validating gold with the exact eval predicate guarantees gold can never score 0 on structural metrics); (c) registry-style invariants ported from `validate_domain`: unique state names, no edge self-loops, every non-terminal state has ≥1 outgoing edge, unique priorities per source state. Reject → repair loop (max 2 attempts, itemized violations fed back — same pattern as `repair_feedback` in `_generate_teacher_conversation`) → drop.

**Rationale:** registry graphs alone would teach 18 vocabularies of state names by heart; invented graphs prevent memorization, and the eval-predicate-as-validator trick makes them as trustworthy as registry graphs at the structural level.

### 2. Generation direction & verification — graph-first inverse rendering + layered faithfulness gate

Direction is fixed: gold graph → teacher writes the playbook (this is the proven `_RICH_PROMPT_SYSTEM` pattern; register 5 literally reuses `build_workflow_script`'s `### [STATE]` renderer with no teacher call at all). Verification per rendering, cheapest first:

| Check | Method | On failure |
|---|---|---|
| State anchor coverage | regex: every canonical state name appears verbatim ≥1× (ASCII, exact) | repair (≤2×, itemized missing anchors) |
| Tool coverage | every tool granted to a state appears verbatim | repair (≤2×) |
| Edge reference coverage | for each edge, the target state's anchor appears within the source state's section/paragraph window; ≥90% of edges must pass, 100% of branch (priority>0) edges | repair (≤2×) |
| Distractor purity | injected distractor paragraphs contain no state anchors / tool names | auto-fix by re-drawing distractor text (no teacher call) |
| Back-extraction spot check | 10% of accepted renderings: a *different-family* teacher extracts a graph from the playbook; score with `evaluate_graph_extraction`; gate node F1 ≥0.90 ∧ edge F1 ≥0.80 | **drop** (a playbook a strong teacher can't decode is ambiguous, not repairable); if >20% of a leg's spot checks fail, halt the leg |

**Rationale:** anchors make coverage checks exact string matching (free, deterministic, works in th/code_switch since anchors stay ASCII); back-extraction is reserved as a sampled statistical gate because it's the only check that catches *semantic* miscommunication, and using a different teacher family avoids correlated blind spots.

### 3. Split hygiene — group split by `graph_id` + two held-out axes

- **Group split:** `scripts/split_task_c_pairs.py` splits at the *graph* level (85/10/5 of graph IDs), then emits all 4–6 renderings of a graph into that graph's split. Never row-level — renderings of one graph are near-duplicates with identical labels.
- **Held-out domain axis:** 2 registry domains — recommend `utilities` and `surveys` (mid-size, not required for L4/L5 eligibility) — appear **only in test**, plus ~20% of invented domain briefs reserved for test. Measures domain OOD.
- **Held-out register axis:** register 6 (manager-explaining transcript) is **excluded from train**; its renderings route to val/test only. Measures register OOD without shrinking train coverage of the other five.

**Rationale:** with 4–6 near-duplicate targets per graph, row-level splitting would leak ~100% of test labels into train; the two held-out axes give the eval a cheap OOD read-out at zero extra generation cost.

### 4. Difficulty knobs & the naming contract

**Naming/ID contract (the scoreability keystone):** `compute_node_f1` and `compute_edge_f1` match on `id` (S\d+), and GED's `node_match` compares `name` labels — so both IDs and names must be deterministic from the playbook text. Contract:

1. **Anchor rule:** every rendering must contain each state's canonical SCREAMING_SNAKE name verbatim at least once — as a heading (`### [VERIFY_IDENTITY]`), an inline parenthetical ("check the customer's ID *(VERIFY_IDENTITY)*"), or a quoted label ("we call this step VERIFY_IDENTITY"). Surrounding body text paraphrases freely and inconsistently — that's the canonicalization skill being trained.
2. **Output names = anchored canonical names**, not the paraphrases.
3. **ID assignment = order of first anchor mention**: S1 = the first-mentioned state, which the rendering contract forces to be the initial state (playbooks must introduce the starting step before any other). Gold JSON per *rendering* re-derives S-numbering from that rendering's mention order (so two registers of the same graph may have permuted IDs — correct, since the pair is (this text, this graph)).
4. Terminal states must be explicitly signposted ("the call ends", "wrap up and close the ticket").

**Difficulty knobs** (recorded as per-sample metadata so difficulty-stratified eval is possible):
- **Distractors:** 30% of renderings get 1–3 irrelevant policy paragraphs (compliance boilerplate, tone guidance, SLA text) with verified anchor/tool purity.
- **Paraphrase density:** low (anchor used throughout) / medium (anchor once, paraphrases after) / high (anchor once, aggressive paraphrase + pronoun reference).
- **Condition explicitness tiers:** explicit ("if the ID fails, go to [ESCALATE]"), narrative-order (priority-0 spine implied by sequencing), and listing-order priority ("first try…, otherwise…, as a last resort…" → model infers `priority` 0/1/2 from bullet order).
- **Explicitly deferred to a v2 hard mode:** unanchored/implicit states — with ID-and-name-based eval matching, an unnamed state has no unique scoreable answer, so it would poison the metrics rather than strengthen them.

### 5. Benchmark split — yes, small and Gemini-cheap

~150 pairs = 25 gold graphs × 6 registers (graphs disjoint from the SFT pool by seed; 5 registry L1–L5 + a handful of invented), languages mixed 50/25/25 en/th/code_switch, teachers `gemini-3-flash` + `gemini-3.1-flash-lite` (two runs, merged — identical rationale to Task A's benchmark: cheap, fast, reliable JSON, and it keeps the GPT legs' fingerprint out of the pre-training ranking data). Written to `data/output/benchmark/task_c/`. Purpose: Phase 1 ranking of 4B–12B candidates with `run_exp_c.sh` before any fine-tuning.

### 6. Teacher leg allocation & cost sketch

| Stage | Leg | Teacher | ~Calls |
|---|---|---|---|
| Graph invention (+repairs) | en only (graphs are ASCII) | `gpt-5.4-mini-2026-03-17` | ~400 |
| Playbook rendering | en (~50%) | `gpt-5.4-mini-2026-03-17` | ~2,900 incl. repairs |
| | th (~30%) | `gemini-3-flash-preview` | ~1,750 |
| | code_switch (~20%) | `gpt-5.4-nano-2026-03-17` | ~1,150 |
| Back-extraction verification (10%) | en/code_switch | `gemini-3-flash` (cross-family vs GPT renderer) | ~350 |
| | th | `gpt-5.4-nano-2026-03-17` (cross-family vs Gemini renderer) | ~150 |
| Benchmark rendering | mixed | `gemini-3-flash` + `gemini-3.1-flash-lite` | ~180 |

Register 5 (`### [state]` script style) costs zero teacher calls — rendered programmatically by `build_workflow_script`. Total ≈ 6,900 calls at ~2k in / ~1.5k out tokens each ≈ 20M tokens; at mini/flash-class pricing this lands in the tens of dollars, an order of magnitude below the Task A SFT run.

### 7. Sample / message format

One JSONL row per (rendering, graph) pair:

```json
{
  "pair_id": "G0142_r3",
  "graph_id": "G0142",
  "source": "registry",                  // or "invented"
  "domain": "insurance",                 // registry key or invented brief slug
  "complexity_level": "L3",              // registry graphs only; "NA" for invented
  "register": "bullet_quick_reference",  // one of the 6 register keys
  "language": "th",
  "num_states": 8, "num_edges": 11,
  "distractor_count": 2,
  "condition_explicitness": "listing_order",
  "verification": {"anchor_coverage": 1.0, "edge_ref_coverage": 0.91,
                   "back_extraction": null},
  "graph": { "nodes": [...], "edges": [...], "initial_state": "S1", "terminal_states": ["S8"] },
  "messages": [
    {"role": "system",    "content": "<fixed extraction system prompt, below>"},
    {"role": "user",      "content": "<the playbook text>"},
    {"role": "assistant", "content": "<json.dumps(graph) — compact, ASCII>"}
  ]
}
```

`graph` duplicates the assistant content in parsed form so eval/cleaning never re-parse the string (same convention as `generate_graph_pairs.py`'s `graph` field). Trained-model system prompt (fixed, English, ~120 tokens):

> You are a workflow-graph extraction engine. Read the playbook and output ONLY a JSON object with keys `nodes`, `edges`, `initial_state`, `terminal_states`. Nodes: `{"id": "S<n>", "name": "<SCREAMING_SNAKE state name as written in the playbook>", "tools": [...], "entry_actions": [...]}`. Number states in order of first mention; S1 is the starting state. Edges: `{"from_state", "to_state", "condition", "priority"}` — priority 0 for the default path, 1+ for alternatives in the order the playbook lists them. Ignore content that does not describe workflow states, transitions, or tools. Output English/ASCII JSON regardless of the playbook language. No markdown fences.

### 8. Intended script interfaces

- `src/llm_workflow_agents/data/generate_playbook_pairs.py` (replaces the weak `generate_graph_pairs.py`, which is kept but deprecated in the doc):
  - `generate_playbook_dataset(num_graphs=850, renderings_per_graph=(4,6), invented_ratio=0.30, language_mix={"en":0.5,"th":0.3,"code_switch":0.2}, render_teachers: dict[str,str], verify_teachers: dict[str,str], back_extraction_rate=0.10, seed=142, output_dir=Path("data/output/sft/task_c")) -> DatasetStats`
  - helpers: `invent_novel_graphs(...)`, `render_playbook(graph, register, language, knobs, teacher) -> str`, `verify_rendering(playbook, graph) -> VerificationReport`, `assign_state_ids(graph, playbook) -> dict` (mention-order S-numbering), `validate_gold_graph(graph)` (imports the eval-module predicates).
- `scripts/generate_playbook_sft_data.sh` — three language legs, `--dry-run` support, per-leg env-key checks (mirrors `generate_sft_data.sh`).
- `scripts/generate_playbook_benchmark_data.sh --teacher <model>` — two Gemini runs + merge (mirrors `generate_benchmark_data_teacher.sh`).
- `scripts/clean_task_c_pairs.py --input-dir data/output/sft/task_c --output-dir data/output/sft/task_c_cleaned` — drops rows failing schema/structural re-validation, dedupes identical playbooks, reports drop stats.
- `scripts/split_task_c_pairs.py --group-key graph_id --heldout-domains utilities,surveys --heldout-registers manager_transcript --seed 142` → `data/output/sft/task_c_splits/{train,validation,test}.jsonl`.
- DVC stages: `task_c_pairs_generate`, `task_c_pairs_clean`, `task_c_pairs_split` (fills the dvc.yaml line-169 placeholder). Eval reuses `src/llm_workflow_agents/eval/graph_extraction_eval.py` via existing `scripts/run_exp_c.sh`.

---

## Worked example (goes in the doc, abbreviated)

**Gold graph** (registry `insurance` L2 subgraph, 5 states): `VERIFY_POLICYHOLDER → CLAIM_INTAKE → ASSESS_COVERAGE → RESOLVE → TERMINAL`, branch `ASSESS_COVERAGE → REQUEST_DOCUMENTATION` (condition "documentation missing", priority 1) with `REQUEST_DOCUMENTATION → ASSESS_COVERAGE` loop-back; tools: `verify_policy` on VERIFY_POLICYHOLDER, `file_claim` on CLAIM_INTAKE.

**Rendering A — formal SOP (en, explicit conditions):**
> **SOP-CL-07: Claims Intake Procedure.** 1.0 VERIFY_POLICYHOLDER. The representative shall confirm the caller's identity using the `verify_policy` tool before any disclosure. Upon successful verification, proceed to 2.0 CLAIM_INTAKE… 3.0 ASSESS_COVERAGE. If documentation is missing, transfer the case to 3.1 REQUEST_DOCUMENTATION and return to 3.0 upon receipt; otherwise continue to 4.0 RESOLVE…

**Rendering B — informal wiki notes (code_switch, listing-order priority, 1 distractor):**
> ทีมเคลม notes 📋 — เริ่มจากเช็คตัวตนลูกค้าก่อนเสมอ (ขั้น VERIFY_POLICYHOLDER, ใช้ verify_policy) แล้วค่อยรับเรื่อง CLAIM_INTAKE ด้วย file_claim… พอถึง ASSESS_COVERAGE: ปกติก็ไป RESOLVE เลย แต่ถ้าเอกสารไม่ครบให้เด้งไป REQUEST_DOCUMENTATION ก่อนแล้วค่อยวนกลับมาเช็คใหม่… *(อย่าลืม: ห้ามรับปากเรื่องยอดเงินก่อน approve — policy ของฝ่าย legal)* ← distractor paragraph, no anchors.

**Gold assistant JSON (both renderings — same topology; IDs follow each rendering's mention order, identical here):** `S1=VERIFY_POLICYHOLDER … S5=REQUEST_DOCUMENTATION`, edge `{"from_state":"S3","to_state":"S5","condition":"documentation missing","priority":1}`, `initial_state":"S1"`, `terminal_states":["S6"]` (TERMINAL mentioned last in both).

---

### Critical Files for Implementation
- /workspaces/ai-agent-llms/docs/data_generation_recipes.md — register/structure template the new doc must mirror
- /workspaces/ai-agent-llms/src/llm_workflow_agents/eval/graph_extraction_eval.py — eval predicates reused as gold-graph validators; source of the ID/name matching constraints that drive the naming contract
- /workspaces/ai-agent-llms/src/llm_workflow_agents/data/generate_workflows.py — `ComplexitySpec` subgraph sampler + `_RICH_PROMPT_SYSTEM`/`_build_rich_prompt_request` inverse-rendering pattern + repair-feedback loop to imitate
- /workspaces/ai-agent-llms/src/llm_workflow_agents/data/generate_graph_pairs.py — existing Task C module being superseded; its `_create_graph_pair` message shape is retained
- /workspaces/ai-agent-llms/scripts/split_task_a_sft.py — CLI/idempotency conventions for the new group-wise `split_task_c_pairs.py`

toolUseResult.prompt:
You are designing a DATA GENERATION RECIPE (a design document, no code will be written now) for the repo at /workspaces/ai-agent-llms. Read docs/data_generation_recipes.md first to match its structure and register — the new doc must feel like a sibling of that file.

## Goal
A recipe for generating training data for a small LLM (4B–12B) that converts a **playbook described in natural language** into a **WorkflowGraph JSON** (the schema in data/templates/graph_output_schema.json: nodes {id S\d+, name, tools, entry_actions}, edges {from_state, to_state, condition, priority}, initial_state, terminal_states). This is effectively a strengthened Task C ("graph extraction").

## User-fixed decisions (do NOT relitigate)
- Output target: WorkflowGraph JSON only (eval reuses eval/graph_extraction_eval.py: node F1 ≥85%, edge F1 ≥75%, GED ≤0.20, JSON validity ≥95%, structural validity ≥90%, mermaid renderability ≥90%).
- Input: multi-register playbook mix — per gold graph, 4–6 NL renderings: (1) formal SOP document, (2) prose paragraphs, (3) bullet-list quick reference, (4) informal training notes/wiki, (5) existing rich `### [state]` prompt style, (6) manager-explaining transcript style.
- Volume: ~5,000 pairs total ≈ 800–1000 unique gold graphs × 4–6 registers. Split 85/10/5.
- Languages: en ~50%, th ~30%, code_switch ~20% (graph JSON output always English/ASCII).
- Deliverable: docs recipe markdown only; generator scripts described with intended interfaces for later implementation.

## Existing assets to build on (from prior exploration)
- `src/llm_workflow_agents/data/domain_registry.py`: 18 domains, each with canonical semantic graph (StateNode(name, instruction, tools), Edge(src,dst,condition,trigger,optional,priority,intent_category)), tools, intents, outbound_reasons; `validate_domain` enforces invariants. Task A's `_select_domain` + ComplexitySpec (L1–L5) subgraph sampler in `generate_workflows.py` already samples subgraphs of varying size (3–20 states, branches, loops, recovery arcs).
- Rich prompt authoring: `_RICH_PROMPT_SYSTEM` + `_build_rich_prompt_request` in `generate_workflows.py` (~lines 1139–1224) — teacher writes playbook-style prose FROM a graph JSON. This graph-first inverse-rendering direction is the proven pattern.
- `_workflow_script.py`: `build_workflow_script` (### [STATE] renderer) + coherence validators (find_tool_placement_violations, find_continuity_violations, find_shape_violations).
- `generate_graph_pairs.py` (existing Task C): weak — NL side is a reused system message; never run to disk; its `_create_graph_pair` message format (system/user/assistant with assistant = json.dumps(graph)) is a reasonable sample shape to keep.
- Teacher client: `_teacher_client.py`; teacher models used elsewhere: gpt-5.4-mini-2026-03-17 (en), gemini-3-flash-preview (th), gpt-5.4-nano-2026-03-17 (code_switch), gemini-3-flash/-3.1-flash-lite (benchmark).
- Seed conventions: benchmark=100, SFT=42 (this new dataset needs a distinct seed).
- Cleanup/split precedent: scripts/clean_task_a_sft.py, scripts/split_task_a_sft.py (deterministic 85/10/5, DVC stages).

## Design questions YOU must resolve with recommendations
1. **Gold graph sourcing**: registry-derived subgraphs (verifiable, free, but only 18 domains) vs teacher-invented novel graphs (OOD generalization, needs validation) — recommend a hybrid ratio and how novel graphs get validated (e.g., structural checks + schema validation + reject/repair loop).
2. **Generation direction & verification**: graph-first inverse rendering (gold graph → teacher writes playbook in register R, language L), plus a **faithfulness verification** step — how to check the playbook actually mentions every state/edge (coverage checks: state-name/paraphrase coverage, edge-condition coverage; possibly a back-extraction spot check with a second teacher). What gets auto-rejected vs repaired.
3. **Split hygiene**: all registers/renderings of one gold graph MUST land in the same split (else near-duplicate leakage). Also consider held-out axes: held-out domains? held-out registers? Recommend a concrete scheme (e.g., 2 domains fully held out to test, 1 register held out to val/test).
4. **Difficulty/realism knobs**: distractor content (irrelevant policy text in playbooks), implicit states (playbook mentions behavior w/o naming a state), inconsistent naming (playbook uses "check the customer's ID" for VERIFY_IDENTITY state — model must canonicalize or invent names; decide the naming contract!), varying explicitness of conditions/priorities. Decide what the naming contract is: does the model output state names verbatim from the playbook, or normalized SCREAMING_SNAKE names? (Eval matches nodes by id/name — think about what's scoreable.)
5. **Benchmark split**: like Task A, do we need a separate small benchmark split (different seed/teachers) for pre-trained model ranking of 4B–12B candidates before fine-tuning? Recommend size/teachers.
6. **Teacher leg allocation & cost sketch**: which teacher per (language leg, stage: graph invention / playbook rendering / verification).
7. **Sample/message format**: exact JSONL sample schema (top-level fields + messages shape) consistent with the repo's conventions; system prompt for the trained model (concise extraction instruction + schema).
8. **Intended script interfaces**: names/CLIs of the future generator scripts (e.g., scripts/generate_playbook_pairs.sh, src/.../data/generate_playbook_pairs.py::generate_playbook_dataset(...)), cleaning/split scripts, mirroring existing conventions.

## Output
Return a detailed design for the recipe document: proposed doc filename + section outline, and for each design question above your concrete recommendation with 1–3 sentences of rationale. Include a small worked example (one gold graph snippet + two register renderings, abbreviated). Keep it implementable-later: every pipeline stage should name its intended module/script and its inputs/outputs.

content.text:
No Write tool is available in this read-only context, so I'll return the complete design directly.

---

# Design: Data Generation Recipe — Task C "Playbook → WorkflowGraph" (5k pairs)

## Proposed document

**Filename:** `/workspaces/ai-agent-llms/docs/data_generation_recipes_task_c.md`
(sibling of `docs/data_generation_recipes.md`; dvc.yaml already reserves "Task B and Task C stages will be added once recipes are defined" at line 169, so this doc also names the DVC stages that fill that placeholder.)

**Section outline** (mirrors the register of `data_generation_recipes.md`: overview table → Shared Concepts → Output Data Format → Recipes → Full Generation Order → Output File Naming):

1. **Header + overview table** — two rows: Benchmark (~150 pairs, Gemini teachers, seed 200) and SFT (~5,000 pairs, GPT+Gemini legs, seed 142; supplies train/val/test via deterministic group split).
2. **Shared Concepts**
   - 2.1 Gold graph sourcing (registry subgraphs vs teacher-invented; validation pipeline)
   - 2.2 The six playbook registers (table with one abbreviated example line each)
   - 2.3 Naming & ID contract (the "anchor rule" — the load-bearing section)
   - 2.4 Difficulty knobs (distractors, paraphrase density, condition explicitness tiers)
   - 2.5 Languages (en/th/code_switch; graph JSON always ASCII)
   - 2.6 Faithfulness verification pipeline (programmatic checks + back-extraction spot check; reject vs repair table)
   - 2.7 Seed allocation, 2.8 Teacher models per stage/leg
3. **Output Data Format** — top-level JSONL fields, messages shape, trained-model system prompt (verbatim), one complete abbreviated sample
4. **Recipes** — Benchmark; SFT; Cleaning; Group-wise train/val/test splits + held-out axes
5. **Worked example** — one gold graph, two register renderings
6. **Full Generation Order** — bash block in the house style
7. **Output File Naming**

---

## Design question resolutions

### 1. Gold graph sourcing — hybrid 70/30

- **~700 registry-derived graphs (70%)**: reuse Task A's `_select_domain` + `ComplexitySpec` L1–L5 subgraph sampler in `src/llm_workflow_agents/data/generate_workflows.py`, with a level mix of L1 10% / L2 25% / L3 35% / L4 20% / L5 10% (Task C benefits from mid-size graphs; tiny L1 graphs saturate quickly). These are free, deterministic, and pre-validated by `validate_domain`.
- **~250–300 teacher-invented graphs (30%)** across ~25 short "domain briefs" deliberately *outside* the 18-domain registry (HR onboarding, IT incident response, loan underwriting, restaurant reservations, warehouse returns, clinical-trial screening, …) to buy OOD generalization. Invention prompt asks for graph JSON only (no prose), 4–14 states.
- **Novel-graph validation**: (a) `jsonschema` validation against `data/templates/graph_output_schema.json`; (b) reuse `check_structural_validity` + `check_mermaid_renderability` from `src/llm_workflow_agents/eval/graph_extraction_eval.py` verbatim (validating gold with the exact eval predicate guarantees gold can never score 0 on structural metrics); (c) registry-style invariants ported from `validate_domain`: unique state names, no edge self-loops, every non-terminal state has ≥1 outgoing edge, unique priorities per source state. Reject → repair loop (max 2 attempts, itemized violations fed back — same pattern as `repair_feedback` in `_generate_teacher_conversation`) → drop.

**Rationale:** registry graphs alone would teach 18 vocabularies of state names by heart; invented graphs prevent memorization, and the eval-predicate-as-validator trick makes them as trustworthy as registry graphs at the structural level.

### 2. Generation direction & verification — graph-first inverse rendering + layered faithfulness gate

Direction is fixed: gold graph → teacher writes the playbook (this is the proven `_RICH_PROMPT_SYSTEM` pattern; register 5 literally reuses `build_workflow_script`'s `### [STATE]` renderer with no teacher call at all). Verification per rendering, cheapest first:

| Check | Method | On failure |
|---|---|---|
| State anchor coverage | regex: every canonical state name appears verbatim ≥1× (ASCII, exact) | repair (≤2×, itemized missing anchors) |
| Tool coverage | every tool granted to a state appears verbatim | repair (≤2×) |
| Edge reference coverage | for each edge, the target state's anchor appears within the source state's section/paragraph window; ≥90% of edges must pass, 100% of branch (priority>0) edges | repair (≤2×) |
| Distractor purity | injected distractor paragraphs contain no state anchors / tool names | auto-fix by re-drawing distractor text (no teacher call) |
| Back-extraction spot check | 10% of accepted renderings: a *different-family* teacher extracts a graph from the playbook; score with `evaluate_graph_extraction`; gate node F1 ≥0.90 ∧ edge F1 ≥0.80 | **drop** (a playbook a strong teacher can't decode is ambiguous, not repairable); if >20% of a leg's spot checks fail, halt the leg |

**Rationale:** anchors make coverage checks exact string matching (free, deterministic, works in th/code_switch since anchors stay ASCII); back-extraction is reserved as a sampled statistical gate because it's the only check that catches *semantic* miscommunication, and using a different teacher family avoids correlated blind spots.

### 3. Split hygiene — group split by `graph_id` + two held-out axes

- **Group split:** `scripts/split_task_c_pairs.py` splits at the *graph* level (85/10/5 of graph IDs), then emits all 4–6 renderings of a graph into that graph's split. Never row-level — renderings of one graph are near-duplicates with identical labels.
- **Held-out domain axis:** 2 registry domains — recommend `utilities` and `surveys` (mid-size, not required for L4/L5 eligibility) — appear **only in test**, plus ~20% of invented domain briefs reserved for test. Measures domain OOD.
- **Held-out register axis:** register 6 (manager-explaining transcript) is **excluded from train**; its renderings route to val/test only. Measures register OOD without shrinking train coverage of the other five.

**Rationale:** with 4–6 near-duplicate targets per graph, row-level splitting would leak ~100% of test labels into train; the two held-out axes give the eval a cheap OOD read-out at zero extra generation cost.

### 4. Difficulty knobs & the naming contract

**Naming/ID contract (the scoreability keystone):** `compute_node_f1` and `compute_edge_f1` match on `id` (S\d+), and GED's `node_match` compares `name` labels — so both IDs and names must be deterministic from the playbook text. Contract:

1. **Anchor rule:** every rendering must contain each state's canonical SCREAMING_SNAKE name verbatim at least once — as a heading (`### [VERIFY_IDENTITY]`), an inline parenthetical ("check the customer's ID *(VERIFY_IDENTITY)*"), or a quoted label ("we call this step VERIFY_IDENTITY"). Surrounding body text paraphrases freely and inconsistently — that's the canonicalization skill being trained.
2. **Output names = anchored canonical names**, not the paraphrases.
3. **ID assignment = order of first anchor mention**: S1 = the first-mentioned state, which the rendering contract forces to be the initial state (playbooks must introduce the starting step before any other). Gold JSON per *rendering* re-derives S-numbering from that rendering's mention order (so two registers of the same graph may have permuted IDs — correct, since the pair is (this text, this graph)).
4. Terminal states must be explicitly signposted ("the call ends", "wrap up and close the ticket").

**Difficulty knobs** (recorded as per-sample metadata so difficulty-stratified eval is possible):
- **Distractors:** 30% of renderings get 1–3 irrelevant policy paragraphs (compliance boilerplate, tone guidance, SLA text) with verified anchor/tool purity.
- **Paraphrase density:** low (anchor used throughout) / medium (anchor once, paraphrases after) / high (anchor once, aggressive paraphrase + pronoun reference).
- **Condition explicitness tiers:** explicit ("if the ID fails, go to [ESCALATE]"), narrative-order (priority-0 spine implied by sequencing), and listing-order priority ("first try…, otherwise…, as a last resort…" → model infers `priority` 0/1/2 from bullet order).
- **Explicitly deferred to a v2 hard mode:** unanchored/implicit states — with ID-and-name-based eval matching, an unnamed state has no unique scoreable answer, so it would poison the metrics rather than strengthen them.

### 5. Benchmark split — yes, small and Gemini-cheap

~150 pairs = 25 gold graphs × 6 registers (graphs disjoint from the SFT pool by seed; 5 registry L1–L5 + a handful of invented), languages mixed 50/25/25 en/th/code_switch, teachers `gemini-3-flash` + `gemini-3.1-flash-lite` (two runs, merged — identical rationale to Task A's benchmark: cheap, fast, reliable JSON, and it keeps the GPT legs' fingerprint out of the pre-training ranking data). Written to `data/output/benchmark/task_c/`. Purpose: Phase 1 ranking of 4B–12B candidates with `run_exp_c.sh` before any fine-tuning.

### 6. Teacher leg allocation & cost sketch

| Stage | Leg | Teacher | ~Calls |
|---|---|---|---|
| Graph invention (+repairs) | en only (graphs are ASCII) | `gpt-5.4-mini-2026-03-17` | ~400 |
| Playbook rendering | en (~50%) | `gpt-5.4-mini-2026-03-17` | ~2,900 incl. repairs |
| | th (~30%) | `gemini-3-flash-preview` | ~1,750 |
| | code_switch (~20%) | `gpt-5.4-nano-2026-03-17` | ~1,150 |
| Back-extraction verification (10%) | en/code_switch | `gemini-3-flash` (cross-family vs GPT renderer) | ~350 |
| | th | `gpt-5.4-nano-2026-03-17` (cross-family vs Gemini renderer) | ~150 |
| Benchmark rendering | mixed | `gemini-3-flash` + `gemini-3.1-flash-lite` | ~180 |

Register 5 (`### [state]` script style) costs zero teacher calls — rendered programmatically by `build_workflow_script`. Total ≈ 6,900 calls at ~2k in / ~1.5k out tokens each ≈ 20M tokens; at mini/flash-class pricing this lands in the tens of dollars, an order of magnitude below the Task A SFT run.

### 7. Sample / message format

One JSONL row per (rendering, graph) pair:

```json
{
  "pair_id": "G0142_r3",
  "graph_id": "G0142",
  "source": "registry",                  // or "invented"
  "domain": "insurance",                 // registry key or invented brief slug
  "complexity_level": "L3",              // registry graphs only; "NA" for invented
  "register": "bullet_quick_reference",  // one of the 6 register keys
  "language": "th",
  "num_states": 8, "num_edges": 11,
  "distractor_count": 2,
  "condition_explicitness": "listing_order",
  "verification": {"anchor_coverage": 1.0, "edge_ref_coverage": 0.91,
                   "back_extraction": null},
  "graph": { "nodes": [...], "edges": [...], "initial_state": "S1", "terminal_states": ["S8"] },
  "messages": [
    {"role": "system",    "content": "<fixed extraction system prompt, below>"},
    {"role": "user",      "content": "<the playbook text>"},
    {"role": "assistant", "content": "<json.dumps(graph) — compact, ASCII>"}
  ]
}
```

`graph` duplicates the assistant content in parsed form so eval/cleaning never re-parse the string (same convention as `generate_graph_pairs.py`'s `graph` field). Trained-model system prompt (fixed, English, ~120 tokens):

> You are a workflow-graph extraction engine. Read the playbook and output ONLY a JSON object with keys `nodes`, `edges`, `initial_state`, `terminal_states`. Nodes: `{"id": "S<n>", "name": "<SCREAMING_SNAKE state name as written in the playbook>", "tools": [...], "entry_actions": [...]}`. Number states in order of first mention; S1 is the starting state. Edges: `{"from_state", "to_state", "condition", "priority"}` — priority 0 for the default path, 1+ for alternatives in the order the playbook lists them. Ignore content that does not describe workflow states, transitions, or tools. Output English/ASCII JSON regardless of the playbook language. No markdown fences.

### 8. Intended script interfaces

- `src/llm_workflow_agents/data/generate_playbook_pairs.py` (replaces the weak `generate_graph_pairs.py`, which is kept but deprecated in the doc):
  - `generate_playbook_dataset(num_graphs=850, renderings_per_graph=(4,6), invented_ratio=0.30, language_mix={"en":0.5,"th":0.3,"code_switch":0.2}, render_teachers: dict[str,str], verify_teachers: dict[str,str], back_extraction_rate=0.10, seed=142, output_dir=Path("data/output/sft/task_c")) -> DatasetStats`
  - helpers: `invent_novel_graphs(...)`, `render_playbook(graph, register, language, knobs, teacher) -> str`, `verify_rendering(playbook, graph) -> VerificationReport`, `assign_state_ids(graph, playbook) -> dict` (mention-order S-numbering), `validate_gold_graph(graph)` (imports the eval-module predicates).
- `scripts/generate_playbook_sft_data.sh` — three language legs, `--dry-run` support, per-leg env-key checks (mirrors `generate_sft_data.sh`).
- `scripts/generate_playbook_benchmark_data.sh --teacher <model>` — two Gemini runs + merge (mirrors `generate_benchmark_data_teacher.sh`).
- `scripts/clean_task_c_pairs.py --input-dir data/output/sft/task_c --output-dir data/output/sft/task_c_cleaned` — drops rows failing schema/structural re-validation, dedupes identical playbooks, reports drop stats.
- `scripts/split_task_c_pairs.py --group-key graph_id --heldout-domains utilities,surveys --heldout-registers manager_transcript --seed 142` → `data/output/sft/task_c_splits/{train,validation,test}.jsonl`.
- DVC stages: `task_c_pairs_generate`, `task_c_pairs_clean`, `task_c_pairs_split` (fills the dvc.yaml line-169 placeholder). Eval reuses `src/llm_workflow_agents/eval/graph_extraction_eval.py` via existing `scripts/run_exp_c.sh`.

---

## Worked example (goes in the doc, abbreviated)

**Gold graph** (registry `insurance` L2 subgraph, 5 states): `VERIFY_POLICYHOLDER → CLAIM_INTAKE → ASSESS_COVERAGE → RESOLVE → TERMINAL`, branch `ASSESS_COVERAGE → REQUEST_DOCUMENTATION` (condition "documentation missing", priority 1) with `REQUEST_DOCUMENTATION → ASSESS_COVERAGE` loop-back; tools: `verify_policy` on VERIFY_POLICYHOLDER, `file_claim` on CLAIM_INTAKE.

**Rendering A — formal SOP (en, explicit conditions):**
> **SOP-CL-07: Claims Intake Procedure.** 1.0 VERIFY_POLICYHOLDER. The representative shall confirm the caller's identity using the `verify_policy` tool before any disclosure. Upon successful verification, proceed to 2.0 CLAIM_INTAKE… 3.0 ASSESS_COVERAGE. If documentation is missing, transfer the case to 3.1 REQUEST_DOCUMENTATION and return to 3.0 upon receipt; otherwise continue to 4.0 RESOLVE…

**Rendering B — informal wiki notes (code_switch, listing-order priority, 1 distractor):**
> ทีมเคลม notes 📋 — เริ่มจากเช็คตัวตนลูกค้าก่อนเสมอ (ขั้น VERIFY_POLICYHOLDER, ใช้ verify_policy) แล้วค่อยรับเรื่อง CLAIM_INTAKE ด้วย file_claim… พอถึง ASSESS_COVERAGE: ปกติก็ไป RESOLVE เลย แต่ถ้าเอกสารไม่ครบให้เด้งไป REQUEST_DOCUMENTATION ก่อนแล้วค่อยวนกลับมาเช็คใหม่… *(อย่าลืม: ห้ามรับปากเรื่องยอดเงินก่อน approve — policy ของฝ่าย legal)* ← distractor paragraph, no anchors.

**Gold assistant JSON (both renderings — same topology; IDs follow each rendering's mention order, identical here):** `S1=VERIFY_POLICYHOLDER … S5=REQUEST_DOCUMENTATION`, edge `{"from_state":"S3","to_state":"S5","condition":"documentation missing","priority":1}`, `initial_state":"S1"`, `terminal_states":["S6"]` (TERMINAL mentioned last in both).

---

### Critical Files for Implementation
- /workspaces/ai-agent-llms/docs/data_generation_recipes.md — register/structure template the new doc must mirror
- /workspaces/ai-agent-llms/src/llm_workflow_agents/eval/graph_extraction_eval.py — eval predicates reused as gold-graph validators; source of the ID/name matching constraints that drive the naming contract
- /workspaces/ai-agent-llms/src/llm_workflow_agents/data/generate_workflows.py — `ComplexitySpec` subgraph sampler + `_RICH_PROMPT_SYSTEM`/`_build_rich_prompt_request` inverse-rendering pattern + repair-feedback loop to imitate
- /workspaces/ai-agent-llms/src/llm_workflow_agents/data/generate_graph_pairs.py — existing Task C module being superseded; its `_create_graph_pair` message shape is retained
- /workspaces/ai-agent-llms/scripts/split_task_a_sft.py — CLI/idempotency conventions for the new group-wise `split_task_c_pairs.py`
The design is complete. Writing the final plan file now.

input.content:
# Plan: Data Generation Recipe — Playbook → WorkflowGraph JSON (Task C strengthened)

## Context

The user wants a data-generation recipe for training a small LLM (4B–12B) that converts a **playbook described in natural language** into a **workflow script**, written as a sibling document to `docs/data_generation_recipes.md`.

Exploration found the repo has both ends of this task but no bridge: the teacher-authored rich playbook path (`_RICH_PROMPT_SYSTEM` in `generate_workflows.py`), the `### [STATE]` script renderer (`_workflow_script.py`), 18 canonical domain graphs (`domain_registry.py`), and graph-extraction eval (`eval/graph_extraction_eval.py`) all exist — but the current Task C generator (`generate_graph_pairs.py`) uses a weak NL side (reused system messages) and has never been run to disk. This recipe fills that gap.

**User-fixed decisions (from brainstorming):**
- Output target: **WorkflowGraph JSON** (schema `data/templates/graph_output_schema.json`); eval reuses `graph_extraction_eval.py` unchanged (node F1 ≥85%, edge F1 ≥75%, GED ≤0.20, JSON/structural validity, Mermaid renderability).
- Input: **multi-register playbook mix** — per gold graph, 4–6 NL renderings: formal SOP, prose paragraphs, bullet quick-reference, informal wiki/training notes, rich `### [state]` style, manager-explaining transcript.
- Volume: **~5,000 pairs** ≈ 850 gold graphs × 4–6 registers, split 85/10/5.
- Languages: **en ~50% / th ~30% / code_switch ~20%** (graph JSON always English/ASCII).
- Deliverable: **recipe markdown only** — no code now; generator scripts described with intended interfaces for later implementation.

## Deliverable

**One new file:** `docs/data_generation_recipes_task_c.md`, mirroring the structure/register of `docs/data_generation_recipes.md` (overview table → Shared Concepts → Output Data Format → Recipes → Worked Example → Full Generation Order → Output File Naming).

No source code, scripts, or config changes. `generate_graph_pairs.py` is noted as superseded/deprecated in the doc, not modified.

## Recipe design (content of the doc)

### 1. Gold graph sourcing — hybrid 70/30
- ~70% **registry-derived subgraphs**: reuse Task A's `_select_domain` + `ComplexitySpec` L1–L5 sampler (`generate_workflows.py`); level mix L1 10 / L2 25 / L3 35 / L4 20 / L5 10.
- ~30% **teacher-invented graphs** across ~25 novel domain briefs outside the registry (HR onboarding, IT incident response, loan underwriting, …) for OOD generalization. Validated by: jsonschema against `graph_output_schema.json` + the eval module's own `check_structural_validity`/Mermaid predicates (gold can never score 0 by construction) + registry-style invariants ported from `validate_domain`. Repair loop (≤2 attempts with itemized violations, same pattern as Task A's `repair_feedback`) → drop.

### 2. Generation direction & faithfulness verification
Graph-first **inverse rendering** (gold graph → teacher writes playbook in register R, language L) — the proven `_RICH_PROMPT_SYSTEM` pattern. Register 5 (`### [state]` style) is rendered programmatically by `build_workflow_script` — zero teacher calls.

Layered verification per rendering, cheapest first:
| Check | Method | On failure |
|---|---|---|
| State anchor coverage | every canonical state name appears verbatim (ASCII regex) | repair ≤2× |
| Tool coverage | every state-granted tool named verbatim | repair ≤2× |
| Edge reference coverage | target anchor appears in source state's section; ≥90% edges, 100% of priority>0 edges | repair ≤2× |
| Distractor purity | distractor paragraphs contain no anchors/tools | re-draw distractor |
| Back-extraction spot check (10%) | cross-family teacher extracts graph; gate node F1 ≥0.90 ∧ edge F1 ≥0.80 via `evaluate_graph_extraction` | drop; halt leg if >20% fail |

### 3. Naming/ID contract (scoreability keystone)
- **Anchor rule:** every rendering contains each state's canonical SCREAMING_SNAKE name verbatim ≥1× (heading, parenthetical, or quoted label); surrounding prose paraphrases freely — canonicalization is the trained skill.
- Output names = anchored canonical names. **ID assignment = order of first mention** (S1 = initial state; per-rendering re-derivation, so registers of one graph may permute IDs — correct, the pair is (this text, this graph)).
- Terminals explicitly signposted. Unanchored/implicit states deferred to v2 (unscoreable under id/name matching).

### 4. Difficulty knobs (recorded as per-sample metadata)
- Distractors: 30% of renderings get 1–3 irrelevant policy paragraphs (purity-verified).
- Paraphrase density: low / medium / high.
- Condition explicitness: explicit / narrative-order / listing-order priority inference.

### 5. Split hygiene
- **Group split by `graph_id`** (85/10/5 of graphs — never row-level; renderings are near-duplicates).
- Held-out **domain** axis: `utilities` + `surveys` (+ ~20% of invented briefs) → test only.
- Held-out **register** axis: manager-transcript register → val/test only.

### 6. Benchmark split
~150 pairs = 25 graphs × 6 registers, seed 200, Gemini teachers (`gemini-3-flash` + `gemini-3.1-flash-lite`, two runs merged) → `data/output/benchmark/task_c/`, for Phase-1-style ranking of 4B–12B candidates before fine-tuning.

### 7. Teacher legs & cost sketch
Graph invention: `gpt-5.4-mini` (~400 calls). Rendering: en `gpt-5.4-mini` / th `gemini-3-flash-preview` / code_switch `gpt-5.4-nano` (~5,800 calls incl. repairs). Back-extraction: cross-family (~500 calls). Total ≈ 6,900 calls ≈ 20M tokens — tens of dollars, well below the Task A SFT run. Seeds: SFT=142, benchmark=200 (disjoint from Task A's 42/100).

### 8. Sample format
One JSONL row per (rendering, graph): `pair_id`, `graph_id`, `source` (registry|invented), `domain`, `complexity_level`, `register`, `language`, counters, difficulty metadata, `verification` report, parsed `graph`, and `messages` [system = fixed ~120-token extraction prompt with the schema + mention-order numbering rule; user = playbook text; assistant = compact ASCII `json.dumps(graph)`] — same shape as `_create_graph_pair` keeps today.

### 9. Intended (future) script interfaces — described in doc, NOT implemented
- `src/llm_workflow_agents/data/generate_playbook_pairs.py::generate_playbook_dataset(num_graphs=850, renderings_per_graph=(4,6), invented_ratio=0.30, language_mix=…, back_extraction_rate=0.10, seed=142, output_dir=data/output/sft/task_c)`
- `scripts/generate_playbook_sft_data.sh` (3 language legs, `--dry-run`), `scripts/generate_playbook_benchmark_data.sh --teacher <model>`
- `scripts/clean_task_c_pairs.py`, `scripts/split_task_c_pairs.py --group-key graph_id --heldout-domains utilities,surveys --heldout-registers manager_transcript --seed 142`
- DVC stages `task_c_pairs_{generate,clean,split}` (fills dvc.yaml's Task C placeholder). Eval: existing `run_exp_c.sh` + `graph_extraction_eval.py`.

### 10. Worked example (in doc)
One insurance L2 gold graph (5 states + branch/loop-back) with two abbreviated renderings — formal SOP (en, explicit conditions) and informal wiki notes (code_switch, listing-order priority, 1 distractor) — plus the shared gold JSON.

## Critical reference files (read-only inputs to doc authoring)
- `docs/data_generation_recipes.md` — structural template
- `src/llm_workflow_agents/eval/graph_extraction_eval.py` — matching semantics driving the naming contract
- `src/llm_workflow_agents/data/generate_workflows.py` (~lines 1139–1224) — rich-prompt inverse-rendering + repair pattern
- `src/llm_workflow_agents/data/_workflow_script.py`, `domain_registry.py`, `generate_graph_pairs.py`, `data/templates/graph_output_schema.json`

## Verification
1. Doc self-review: no TBDs; every pipeline stage names its module/script + inputs/outputs; naming contract consistent with `graph_extraction_eval.py` matching (nodes by id/name, edges by from/to).
2. Cross-check numbers: register×graph×language arithmetic sums to ~5,000; splits sum to 100%; seeds disjoint from 42/100.
3. Worked-example gold JSON validates mentally against `graph_output_schema.json` (required keys, S\d+ ids, terminal list non-empty).
4. Commit the doc (markdown-only commit; per user's git-commit preference markdown is normally excluded — this deliverable IS markdown, so commit it explicitly and verify branch first per memory `feedback_verify_branch_before_commit`).

# Plan: Data Generation Recipe — Playbook → WorkflowGraph JSON (Task C strengthened)

## Context

The user wants a data-generation recipe for training a small LLM (4B–12B) that converts a **playbook described in natural language** into a **workflow script**, written as a sibling document to `docs/data_generation_recipes.md`.

Exploration found the repo has both ends of this task but no bridge: the teacher-authored rich playbook path (`_RICH_PROMPT_SYSTEM` in `generate_workflows.py`), the `### [STATE]` script renderer (`_workflow_script.py`), 18 canonical domain graphs (`domain_registry.py`), and graph-extraction eval (`eval/graph_extraction_eval.py`) all exist — but the current Task C generator (`generate_graph_pairs.py`) uses a weak NL side (reused system messages) and has never been run to disk. This recipe fills that gap.

**User-fixed decisions (from brainstorming):**
- Output target: **WorkflowGraph JSON** (schema `data/templates/graph_output_schema.json`); eval reuses `graph_extraction_eval.py` unchanged (node F1 ≥85%, edge F1 ≥75%, GED ≤0.20, JSON/structural validity, Mermaid renderability).
- Input: **multi-register playbook mix** — per gold graph, 4–6 NL renderings: formal SOP, prose paragraphs, bullet quick-reference, informal wiki/training notes, rich `### [state]` style, manager-explaining transcript.
- Volume: **~5,000 pairs** ≈ 850 gold graphs × 4–6 registers, split 85/10/5.
- Languages: **en ~50% / th ~30% / code_switch ~20%** (graph JSON always English/ASCII).
- Deliverable: **recipe markdown only** — no code now; generator scripts described with intended interfaces for later implementation.

## Deliverable

**One new file:** `docs/data_generation_recipes_task_c.md`, mirroring the structure/register of `docs/data_generation_recipes.md` (overview table → Shared Concepts → Output Data Format → Recipes → Worked Example → Full Generation Order → Output File Naming).

No source code, scripts, or config changes. `generate_graph_pairs.py` is noted as superseded/deprecated in the doc, not modified.

## Recipe design (content of the doc)

### 1. Gold graph sourcing — hybrid 70/30
- ~70% **registry-derived subgraphs**: reuse Task A's `_select_domain` + `ComplexitySpec` L1–L5 sampler (`generate_workflows.py`); level mix L1 10 / L2 25 / L3 35 / L4 20 / L5 10.
- ~30% **teacher-invented graphs** across ~25 novel domain briefs outside the registry (HR onboarding, IT incident response, loan underwriting, …) for OOD generalization. Validated by: jsonschema against `graph_output_schema.json` + the eval module's own `check_structural_validity`/Mermaid predicates (gold can never score 0 by construction) + registry-style invariants ported from `validate_domain`. Repair loop (≤2 attempts with itemized violations, same pattern as Task A's `repair_feedback`) → drop.

### 2. Generation direction & faithfulness verification
Graph-first **inverse rendering** (gold graph → teacher writes playbook in register R, language L) — the proven `_RICH_PROMPT_SYSTEM` pattern. Register 5 (`### [state]` style) is rendered programmatically by `build_workflow_script` — zero teacher calls.

Layered verification per rendering, cheapest first:
| Check | Method | On failure |
|---|---|---|
| State anchor coverage | every canonical state name appears verbatim (ASCII regex) | repair ≤2× |
| Tool coverage | every state-granted tool named verbatim | repair ≤2× |
| Edge reference coverage | target anchor appears in source state's section; ≥90% edges, 100% of priority>0 edges | repair ≤2× |
| Distractor purity | distractor paragraphs contain no anchors/tools | re-draw distractor |
| Back-extraction spot check (10%) | cross-family teacher extracts graph; gate node F1 ≥0.90 ∧ edge F1 ≥0.80 via `evaluate_graph_extraction` | drop; halt leg if >20% fail |

### 3. Naming/ID contract (scoreability keystone)
- **Anchor rule:** every rendering contains each state's canonical SCREAMING_SNAKE name verbatim ≥1× (heading, parenthetical, or quoted label); surrounding prose paraphrases freely — canonicalization is the trained skill.
- Output names = anchored canonical names. **ID assignment = order of first mention** (S1 = initial state; per-rendering re-derivation, so registers of one graph may permute IDs — correct, the pair is (this text, this graph)).
- Terminals explicitly signposted. Unanchored/implicit states deferred to v2 (unscoreable under id/name matching).

### 4. Difficulty knobs (recorded as per-sample metadata)
- Distractors: 30% of renderings get 1–3 irrelevant policy paragraphs (purity-verified).
- Paraphrase density: low / medium / high.
- Condition explicitness: explicit / narrative-order / listing-order priority inference.

### 5. Split hygiene
- **Group split by `graph_id`** (85/10/5 of graphs — never row-level; renderings are near-duplicates).
- Held-out **domain** axis: `utilities` + `surveys` (+ ~20% of invented briefs) → test only.
- Held-out **register** axis: manager-transcript register → val/test only.

### 6. Benchmark split
~150 pairs = 25 graphs × 6 registers, seed 200, Gemini teachers (`gemini-3-flash` + `gemini-3.1-flash-lite`, two runs merged) → `data/output/benchmark/task_c/`, for Phase-1-style ranking of 4B–12B candidates before fine-tuning.

### 7. Teacher legs & cost sketch
Graph invention: `gpt-5.4-mini` (~400 calls). Rendering: en `gpt-5.4-mini` / th `gemini-3-flash-preview` / code_switch `gpt-5.4-nano` (~5,800 calls incl. repairs). Back-extraction: cross-family (~500 calls). Total ≈ 6,900 calls ≈ 20M tokens — tens of dollars, well below the Task A SFT run. Seeds: SFT=142, benchmark=200 (disjoint from Task A's 42/100).

### 8. Sample format
One JSONL row per (rendering, graph): `pair_id`, `graph_id`, `source` (registry|invented), `domain`, `complexity_level`, `register`, `language`, counters, difficulty metadata, `verification` report, parsed `graph`, and `messages` [system = fixed ~120-token extraction prompt with the schema + mention-order numbering rule; user = playbook text; assistant = compact ASCII `json.dumps(graph)`] — same shape as `_create_graph_pair` keeps today.

### 9. Intended (future) script interfaces — described in doc, NOT implemented
- `src/llm_workflow_agents/data/generate_playbook_pairs.py::generate_playbook_dataset(num_graphs=850, renderings_per_graph=(4,6), invented_ratio=0.30, language_mix=…, back_extraction_rate=0.10, seed=142, output_dir=data/output/sft/task_c)`
- `scripts/generate_playbook_sft_data.sh` (3 language legs, `--dry-run`), `scripts/generate_playbook_benchmark_data.sh --teacher <model>`
- `scripts/clean_task_c_pairs.py`, `scripts/split_task_c_pairs.py --group-key graph_id --heldout-domains utilities,surveys --heldout-registers manager_transcript --seed 142`
- DVC stages `task_c_pairs_{generate,clean,split}` (fills dvc.yaml's Task C placeholder). Eval: existing `run_exp_c.sh` + `graph_extraction_eval.py`.

### 10. Worked example (in doc)
One insurance L2 gold graph (5 states + branch/loop-back) with two abbreviated renderings — formal SOP (en, explicit conditions) and informal wiki notes (code_switch, listing-order priority, 1 distractor) — plus the shared gold JSON.

## Critical reference files (read-only inputs to doc authoring)
- `docs/data_generation_recipes.md` — structural template
- `src/llm_workflow_agents/eval/graph_extraction_eval.py` — matching semantics driving the naming contract
- `src/llm_workflow_agents/data/generate_workflows.py` (~lines 1139–1224) — rich-prompt inverse-rendering + repair pattern
- `src/llm_workflow_agents/data/_workflow_script.py`, `domain_registry.py`, `generate_graph_pairs.py`, `data/templates/graph_output_schema.json`

## Verification
1. Doc self-review: no TBDs; every pipeline stage names its module/script + inputs/outputs; naming contract consistent with `graph_extraction_eval.py` matching (nodes by id/name, edges by from/to).
2. Cross-check numbers: register×graph×language arithmetic sums to ~5,000; splits sum to 100%; seeds disjoint from 42/100.
3. Worked-example gold JSON validates mentally against `graph_output_schema.json` (required keys, S\d+ ids, terminal list non-empty).
4. Commit the doc (markdown-only commit; per user's git-commit preference markdown is normally excluded — this deliverable IS markdown, so commit it explicitly and verify branch first per memory `feedback_verify_branch_before_commit`).

100	        return None
101	
102	
103	def compute_node_f1(
104	    predicted: WorkflowGraph,
105	    gold: WorkflowGraph,
106	) -> float:
107	    """Compute F1 score for node prediction.
108	
109	    Matches nodes by ID. A predicted node matches if its ID exists
110	    in the gold graph.
111	    """
112	    pred_ids = {n.get("id", n.get("name", "")) for n in predicted.nodes}
113	    gold_ids = {n.get("id", n.get("name", "")) for n in gold.nodes}
114	
115	    if not pred_ids and not gold_ids:
116	        return 1.0
117	    if not pred_ids or not gold_ids:
118	        return 0.0
119	
120	    tp = len(pred_ids & gold_ids)
121	    precision = tp / len(pred_ids)
122	    recall = tp / len(gold_ids)
123	
124	    if precision + recall == 0:
125	        return 0.0
126	    return 2 * precision * recall / (precision + recall)
127	
128	
129	def compute_edge_f1(
130	    predicted: WorkflowGraph,
131	    gold: WorkflowGraph,
132	) -> float:
133	    """Compute F1 score for edge prediction.
134	
135	    Matches edges by (from_state, to_state) pair.
136	    """
137	    pred_edges = {
138	        (e.get("from_state", ""), e.get("to_state", ""))
139	        for e in predicted.edges
140	    }
141	    gold_edges = {
142	        (e.get("from_state", ""), e.get("to_state", ""))
143	        for e in gold.edges
144	    }
145	
146	    if not pred_edges and not gold_edges:
147	        return 1.0
148	    if not pred_edges or not gold_edges:
149	        return 0.0
150	
151	    tp = len(pred_edges & gold_edges)
152	    precision = tp / len(pred_edges)
153	    recall = tp / len(gold_edges)
154	
155	    if precision + recall == 0:
156	        return 0.0
157	    return 2 * precision * recall / (precision + recall)
158	
159	
160	def compute_graph_edit_distance(
161	    predicted: WorkflowGraph,
162	    gold: WorkflowGraph,
163	    normalize: bool = True,
164	) -> float:
165	    """Compute Graph Edit Distance using networkx.
166	
167	    Args:
168	        predicted: Predicted workflow graph.
169	        gold: Gold-standard workflow graph.
170	        normalize: If True, normalize by max(|V_pred|+|E_pred|, |V_gold|+|E_gold|).
171	
172	    Returns:
173	        GED (normalized to [0, 1] if normalize=True).
174	    """
175	    import networkx as nx
176	
177	    g_pred = _to_networkx(predicted)
178	    g_gold = _to_networkx(gold)
179	
180	    # Use approximate GED for efficiency (exact GED is NP-hard)
181	    try:
182	        ged = nx.graph_edit_distance(
183	            g_pred,
184	            g_gold,
185	            node_match=lambda a, b: a.get("label") == b.get("label"),
186	            timeout=5,
187	        )
188	        if ged is None:
189	            ged = float(abs(len(g_pred) - len(g_gold)) + abs(g_pred.number_of_edges() - g_gold.number_of_edges()))
190	    except Exception:
191	        # Fallback: simple structural difference
192	        ged = float(
193	            abs(len(g_pred) - len(g_gold))
194	            + abs(g_pred.number_of_edges() - g_gold.number_of_edges())
195	        )
196	
197	    if normalize:
198	        max_size = max(
199	            len(g_pred) + g_pred.number_of_edges(),
200	            len(g_gold) + g_gold.number_of_edges(),
201	            1,
202	        )
203	        return min(ged / max_size, 1.0)
204	
205	    return ged
206	
207	
208	def _to_networkx(graph: WorkflowGraph) -> "nx.DiGraph":
209	    """Convert WorkflowGraph to networkx DiGraph."""
210	    import networkx as nx
211	
212	    g = nx.DiGraph()
213	    for node in graph.nodes:
214	        node_id = node.get("id", node.get("name", ""))
215	        g.add_node(node_id, label=node.get("name", node_id))
216	
217	    for edge in graph.edges:
218	        src = edge.get("from_state", "")
219	        dst = edge.get("to_state", "")
220	        if src and dst:
221	            g.add_edge(src, dst, condition=edge.get("condition", ""))
222	
223	    return g
